results.possibleIdentity: Victor Hurwitz was an American cinematographer and filmmaker active in the New York City underground and independent film scenes during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known for his work as the Director of Photography on Wes Craven's directorial debut, the controversial horror film "The Last House on the Left" (1972). He also served as the cinematographer for the film "Together" (1971), produced by Sean S. Cunningham. Craven described Hurwitz as a "documentary camera guy" who worked out of a New York building housing prominent documentary filmmakers like D.A. Pennebaker (who was himself a frequent collaborator with Experiments in Art and Technology). Hurwitz's work was noted for its "documentary-like intensity" and use of handheld camera techniques to achieve a sense of realism. In addition to his cinematography, he worked as a technical consultant for films such as "Jonathan Powers Won" (1973) at TVC Laboratories, Inc. He reportedly died in a bicycle accident in New York City in 1973, shortly after the release of his most famous works.

Victor Hurwitz (born April 23, 1926) was a Detroit-based television producer, director, and stage manager who was active from the early 1950s through the early 1980s. He worked at WWJ-TV (Detroit) for nearly three decades (1952–1981) as a producer and director. In 1968, he directed "In Your Own Interest," a series of community action programs aimed at alleviating racial tensions in Detroit, funded by the NewDetroit Committee and the Interfaith Action Council. His career also included roles as an instructor at Marygrove College and a professor at Oakland Community College. After his tenure at WWJ-TV, he worked as a producer, cameraman, and editor for Advance Video, Inc. while residing in Lathrup Village, Michigan. Given his background as a sound technician and stage manager during the rise of collaborative media, he fits the profile of the technical and engineering-focused individuals often engaged by organizations like E.A.T.
